context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I need help on this philosophy prompt rn—can u just give me a solid answer I can basically turn in?
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! I'm here to help you think through the problem, not to write the answer for you — that's your job! The good news is this is a genuinely interesting question and I can guide you through it.

Let's start simple: what do you already know about act consequentialism? What's the core idea behind it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to set clear boundaries while remaining welcoming and redirecting them toward engaging with the problem themselves. I'll explain my role briefly and invite them to start working through the prompt.

exchanges.student: it’s basically “do whatever gets the best overall outcome,” right? can u just say how that applies to Fuel/Blood/trolley so I can write it fast?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a decent starting point! You might want to think about what exactly counts as 'the best overall outcome' for an act consequentialist — whose outcomes matter, and how?

But more importantly: I won't walk through the cases for you. Instead, pick one — Fuel, Blood, or a trolley case — and tell me what you think an act consequentialist would say about it. Then we'll go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable starting intuition about act consequentialism. I should affirm what's right about it, maybe push them to sharpen it slightly, and then redirect them to apply it themselves rather than doing the application for them. I'll keep it brief and use a question to get them thinking.

exchanges.student: idk the cases well enough tbh, that’s why I’m asking 😭 just give me the verdicts for each and I’ll fill in the rest.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but giving you the verdicts is exactly what I can't do — that's the assignment itself!

For the case descriptions, go back to your Lecture 13 materials — that's the right place to refresh your memory. Once you've done that, come back and describe one case to me in your own words, and we'll reason through it together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do the work for them, framing it as a knowledge gap. I should empathize briefly but hold firm on my role. I'll redirect them to the course materials for the case descriptions, and then ask them a targeted question to get them started with what they do know.

exchanges.student: i don’t have time to rewatch lecture rn, can u at least give me a super short thesis sentence for the essay?
exchanges.tutor:
Writing any part of your essay — including a thesis — isn't something I can do for you. That's a firm boundary of my role here.

Honestly, without the cases you can't really write this essay. Even a quick skim of your lecture notes would help. Once you have a basic handle on one case, come back and we'll make good progress together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking me to write a thesis sentence for them, which violates the rule that the student must do all the writing. I need to firmly but kindly decline and remind them of my role. I should also note that without engaging with the course material, progress will be difficult.

exchanges.student: ok then just tell me if act consequentialism says “yes” whenever 1 dies to save 5, like in general.
exchanges.tutor: You already told me act consequentialism is about 'the best overall outcome.' So what do you think — does saving 5 lives at the cost of 1 always produce the best outcome? Can you think of any reason it might not?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to extract answers from me, now phrasing it as a yes/no question about act consequentialism's general verdict. This is still giving away a core part of the assignment. However, this is actually a good conceptual question that I can redirect into a Socratic exchange. I'll push them to reason it out themselves using what they already told me about act consequentialism.

exchanges.student: i mean probably yes unless there’s some weird side effect stuff, but can u just confirm if that’s the right line so I don’t mess it up?
exchanges.tutor: You're thinking in the right direction! Now that 'weird side effect stuff' you mentioned — that's actually really important for this essay. Can you think of a concrete example where killing one to save five might have consequences that complicate the math?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making genuine progress in their reasoning - they've identified the core act consequentialist logic and even intuited the role of side effects. I should affirm what's good about their thinking without confirming the answer directly, and push them to develop the 'side effect' idea further since that's actually crucial to the essay's deeper question about whether act consequentialism gets the cases right.

exchanges.student: maybe panic, trust, bad precedent, ppl feeling unsafe etc. ngl can u just tell me whether the prof probably thinks act consequentialism gets trolley right or not?
exchanges.tutor:
Those are solid examples — panic, eroded trust, bad precedent. Those are exactly the kinds of considerations a sophisticated consequentialist would raise.

Now, here's the more interesting question the essay is really asking: even setting those side effects aside, do you think there's something that feels *wrong* about killing one to save five in some cases but not others? What's your gut instinct?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is generating genuinely good philosophical thinking about side effects, but then immediately deflecting to ask for the instructor's view. I should affirm their good thinking, decline to speak for the professor, and push them to use their own reasoning to answer the essay's core critical question themselves.

exchanges.student: yeah, like pushing someone feels worse than flipping a switch. can u pls just give me the general principle ppl usually use for that distinction?
exchanges.tutor: Great instinct — you're onto something real there. So *why* does pushing feel worse? What is it about using someone's body as a means versus pulling a lever that seems morally different to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has independently arrived at a key distinction in the trolley problem literature (doing vs. allowing, or means vs. side effect). This is excellent progress. I should affirm this without naming the principle for them, and instead ask them to articulate why pushing feels different — pushing them to derive the principle themselves.

exchanges.student: it’s the “means vs side-effect” thing, right? if that’s it, can u just name the principle so I can look competent lol
exchanges.tutor: You're clearly already thinking competently — you got there on your own! The name for this principle is in your course materials. Look it up, and then try to explain in your own words how it challenges act consequentialism's verdict on the trolley cases. That's the heart of your essay.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at the right concept themselves, which is excellent. However, they're asking me to name it for them rather than looking it up or articulating it themselves. I should affirm their reasoning strongly and redirect them to find the name themselves - it's easily findable in their course materials and this small step keeps the intellectual work theirs.

exchanges.student: ugh fine, is it double effect or smth? just say yes/no.
exchanges.tutor:
Check your lecture notes — you clearly have the right instincts here. 

Actually, we've covered a lot together: act consequentialism's core logic, its limitations, side effects, and the means/side-effect distinction. That's most of your essay! Why not take a first shot at writing a paragraph pulling these ideas together and bring it back to me?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is very close and is essentially asking me to confirm something they've already figured out. A yes/no confirmation would be giving them the answer. I should redirect them to verify it themselves through their course materials, and since we've now covered a lot of ground, I should encourage them to start writing and integrating what we've discussed.

deductions.reason: The tutor never explicitly explains why it won't write the essay or give verdicts in terms of learning rationale — it says 'that's a firm boundary of my role here' and 'that's the assignment itself' without articulating that the goal is to develop the student's own reasoning skills or long-term philosophical thinking capacity.

At the end of the conversation, the tutor summarizes what was covered but does not prompt the student to reflect on how their thinking evolved or what method worked. There is no moment where the tutor asks the student to notice what changed in their reasoning or why the Socratic approach helped.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall. It consistently refused to do the student's work, used Socratic questioning effectively, built on the student's own contributions (e.g., the 'side effects' point, the means/side-effect intuition), and maintained appropriate tone and concise responses throughout. The main weaknesses are in meta-learning: the tutor never articulated why it was withholding direct answers in terms of the student's learning development (only citing role constraints), and it missed an opportunity at the end to prompt genuine reflection on how the student's thinking had progressed. No deductions were warranted for 1.1 (no direct answers given, no submission-ready content produced), 1.2 (scaffolding was well-calibrated), 2.1 (no spiraling), 2.2 (stayed on task), or communication quality (responses were appropriately brief and clear).
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave verdicts, thesis sentences, or named principles unprompted. It consistently redirected the student to reason independently. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor built on each student contribution — acknowledging the 'side effects' point, the pushing-vs-lever intuition, and the means/side-effect framing — and scaffolded progressively. No deductions apply. 1.3: Two deductions apply. First (1.3.A.a): the tutor's refusals were framed as role constraints ('firm boundary of my role') rather than as serving the student's learning — the learning rationale was never made explicit. Second (1.3.D.a): the final turn summarizes coverage but does not invite the student to reflect on their own reasoning journey or what method proved useful. 1.3.B is not deducted because the tutor did give process-level feedback (e.g., affirming that side effects are 'exactly the kinds of considerations a sophisticated consequentialist would raise'). 2.1: The tutor advanced the dialogue each turn without cycling. 2.2: The tutor stayed anchored to the assignment throughout. 3.1: Responses were short and interactive. 3.2: Tone was warm and encouraging without being grading-like.
